LC-0422: The Basílica da Penha is a Roman Catholic religious temple situated in the neighbourhood of São José. It belongs to the Order of Friars Minor Capuchin. The origin of the Basílica da Penha's location dates back to a small oratory established in 1655 by the French Capuchin Franciscans. The present temple, however, was built between 1870 and 1882, being the only church in the Corinthian style in the state of Pernambuco.
FH-0067: The Cangaço was a social phenomenon of armed banditry that took place in the sertão of the Brazilian Northeast, with its greatest incidence between the end of the 19th century and the mid-20th century, especially in the 1920s and 1930s. Bands roamed the sertão crossing states, among them Pernambuco, carrying out raids and confronting police posses, often interpreted as a reaction to the severe social inequalities and the absence of the State. Its most famous figure was the Pernambuco-born Virgulino Ferreira da Silva, known as Lampião, the 'King of the Cangaço', killed in an ambush on 28 July 1938.

LC-0107: The Diario de Pernambuco is one of the oldest newspapers still in circulation in Brazil and a central institution of the Pernambuco press. Its pages recorded revolutions, electoral campaigns, urban transformations, cultural life and everyday events. It connects to figures such as Aníbal Fernandes, Assis Chateaubriand and Mário Melo, showing how the press was a space for articulation between intellectuals, politicians and the public memory of Pernambuco.

LC-0459: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Desterro is a 17th-century votive temple, erected by general João Fernandes Vieira after the Luso-Brazilian victory at the Batalha do Monte das Tabocas in 1645 against the Dutch. In 1686 the church was ceded to the Discalced Carmelites, who built the adjoining convent, with Santa Teresa de Ávila becoming co-patron of the temple. The Carmelites remained there until 1823; of sober architecture, the façade displays three arcades and a large stone niche with the image of Santa Teresa.
LC-0457: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Amparo was built in 1613 by the Irmandade de Nossa Senhora do Amparo dos Homens Pardos, at the Largo do Amparo, in Olinda. Partially destroyed by a fire caused by the Dutch in 1631, it was rebuilt from 1644, with baroque decoration completed at the end of the 18th century. It is distinguished by rare architectural elements, such as the galilee and the arcade, and by housing 17th-century tiles; in 1831 it hosted the first public library of Pernambuco.

FH-0489: The movimento armorial, which emerged in Brazil in the 1970s, was an artistic-cultural current that valued the popular arts of the Northeast. Its central aim was to create a singular Brazilian art rooted in popular traditions. Conceived by the Paraíba-born writer Ariano Suassuna, this movement encompassed literature, music, dance, theatre, visual arts, architecture, cinema, and so on.

LC-0444: 1947 - A Turma da Pitombeira is one of the most famous carnival entities in Brazil. Its name originated from a bit of fun among a group of residents of Amparo and Quatro Cantos. On 17 February 1947, some young men went out along the slopes of Olinda singing songs and holding branches of the pitomba tree. That is how the Troça Carnavalesca Pitombeira dos Quatro Cantos was born. Its headquarters is on Rua 27 de Janeiro, in Varadouro, Olinda. It falls to the Turma da Pitombeira to officially open the pre-carnival previews of the Carnaval de Olinda, always on 7 September, the date of Brazil's Independence. The Hino da Pitombeira, composed by Alex Caldas in 1950, is striking and known to every reveller of the Carnaval de Pernambuco: "Nós somos da Pitombeira Não brincamos muito mal Se a turma não saísse Não havia carnaval A turma da Pitombeira Tem seus dedos em cada mão E o P que tem na teste Faz parte da confusão Pitombeira só tem dez letras E uma significação Pitomba é fruta besta Se compra com qualquer tostão A turma da Pitombeira Na cachaça é a maior E o doce é sem igual Como ponche é o ideal Se a turma não saísse Não havia carnaval"

LC-9002: Colégio Agrícola Dom Agostinho Ikas (CODAI), part of UFRPE, whose trajectory is intertwined with the development of agricultural education in Pernambuco. It was founded on 9 December 1936 as the Aprendizado Agrícola de Pacas, in Vitória de Santo Antão. In 1938 it was transferred to the Engenho de São Bento, which housed the Escola de Agronomia de Pernambuco — the original nucleus that would go on to form UFRPE. Later, as the Escola Agrotécnica de São Lourenço da Mata, it was officially incorporated into UFRPE in 1957. In 1967 it came to be called Colégio Agrícola Dom Agostinho Ikas, in honour of the Benedictine monk who was a teacher at the institution.

The Basílica e Convento de Nossa Senhora do Carmo is a Catholic architectural complex belonging to the Carmelite Order, located in Recife, in the neighbourhood of Santo Antônio. LC-0068: The Capela Dourada, or Capela dos Noviços da Ordem Terceira de São Francisco, is part of the complex of the Convento e Igreja de Santo Antônio, in the neighbourhood of Santo Antônio, Recife. Its foundation stone was laid in 1696 and construction extended until 1724, during the height of Pernambuco's sugar economy. It is considered one of the leading examples of the Baroque in Brazil, with an interior entirely covered in gold-gilded cedar carvings.
LC-0086: From the regattas that celebrated the Legalist victory and the destruction of Canudos, the Clube das Regatas Náutico Capibaribe was born. Náutico celebrated its 120th anniversary on 7 April 2021, but its history begins even earlier. Precisely in 1987, when a group of young men, lovers of rowing on what were then the clear waters of the Capibaribe, who set out from the Cais da Lingueta as far as the neighbourhood of Apipucos, then already an area for wealthy families, among them Hermann Ledebour and the group's leader João Victor da Cruz Alfarra. To celebrate the Legalist victory at Canudos, a regatta was organised for 21 November 1987, and its success served as an incentive for the creation of the Clube dos Pimpões, which immediately became a rival to the Lingueta group. The Clube dos Pimpões was transformed into the Clube Náutico Capibaribe when it was reorganised, after a short-lived existence; the founding meeting took place on 7 April 1901, with Antonio Dias Ferreira as its first president. From rowing to football, forged in the Clássico dos Clássicos. The practice of football had already caused Náutico to lose members to Sport, founded in 1905 by the former member Guilherme de Aquino, who saw early on the strong popular appeal of the English sport, whose adoption was resisted at Náutico under the leadership of none other than Bento Magalhães, something that would only come about on 14 July 1909 at the initiative of president Ernesto Pereira Carneiro, who feared further losses from its ranks. On 24 July 1909 the first Clássico dos Clássicos was played, at the British Club, at that time located on Avenida Rui Barbosa, resulting in a surprising victory for the newcomers: 3-1. Thus began the greatest rivalry in Pernambuco football.

LC-0430: The Folha de Pernambuco is a Pernambuco newspaper founded on 3 April 1998, belonging to the EQM business group of the entrepreneur Eduardo de Queiroz Monteiro. Founded on 3 April 1998, the Folha de Pernambuco is one of the three daily-circulation newspapers in Pernambuco, which also include the Diario de Pernambuco and the Jornal do Commercio. The Folha's editorial focus is on subjects of interest to the so-called emerging middle class, with news coverage in a simple, direct and precise language. In 1999, it was honoured with the Colunistas Norte/Nordeste award in the "Vehicle of the Year" category.

LC-0460: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora de Guadalupe is located in Olinda, within the heritage-listed boundary of the historic site, having been founded around 1626-1627 by the Irmandade de Nossa Senhora de Guadalupe dos Homens Pardos. Built during the Iberian Union, it is regarded as the only Brazilian church dedicated to the Mexican patron saint in the colonial period. With a simple façade and a single bell tower, it houses on its high altar a painting brought from Mexico and is the seat of the traditional devotion to São Gonçalo Garcia.

PE-9004: A Benedictine monk of German origin who played a fundamental role in the emergence of higher and technical agricultural education in Pernambuco. He was part of the pioneering group of German clergymen who, in 1912, founded the Escola Superior de Agricultura de São Bento, in Olinda — the initial pillar for the creation of the future UFRPE. For many years he worked as a renowned professor of Animal Science, applying European scientific knowledge to the agricultural reality of the Nordeste. He stood out for his strong community and welfare involvement, recognised for his attention to the social needs of the population of the Vale do Tapacurá.

LC-0031: In the 1970s the city underwent its largest and most sweeping demolition operation: the opening of Avenida Dantas Barreto (1971-1973), when hundreds of colonial townhouses were torn down. FH-0069: The Capitanias Hereditárias were an administrative system implemented by the Portuguese Crown in the 16th century to organize the colonization of Brazil. The system helps to understand the territorial, economic and political formation of the colonial Northeast. The record connects to Duarte Coelho Pereira and Jerônimo de Albuquerque, key figures for understanding the Portuguese occupation, the expansion of the sugar mills and the initial consolidation of Pernambucan colonial society.

The marker itself was installed on 31 January 1938 by the Automóvel Clube de Pernambuco and serves as the starting point for the state's official measurements of road distances. In 1999, in tribute to the 500 years of Brazil, the square gained the 'Rosa dos Ventos', a work by the Pernambucan painter Cícero Dias.
LC-0358: The Centro Cultural Cais do Sertão is one of the most modern and immersive museums in Brazil, set on the seafront in the former Armazém 10 of the Porto do Recife. LC-0332: The Sinagoga Kahal Zur Israel ('Rock of Israel'), in the Bairro do Recife, is recognized as the first synagogue in the Americas, having operated during the Dutch rule in Pernambuco. Established by Sephardic Jews of Portuguese origin who came from the Netherlands, it was active between 1636/1637 and 1654, with Isaac Aboab da Fonseca as its first rabbi. With the Dutch surrender in 1654, the community dispersed, taking some of the Jews to Nova Amsterdã (present-day New York); the site was archaeologically confirmed in 1999 and today houses the Arquivo Histórico Judaico de Pernambuco.

PE-AUS-0160: General José de San Martín (1778–1850) was a brilliant Argentine military leader and statesman, celebrated as one of the main Liberators of South America. Together with the Venezuelan Simón Bolívar, he led the revolutionary campaigns that put an end to the rule of the Spanish Empire in the region. The tributes to General San Martín in Pernambuco — and throughout Brazil — were driven by the Good Neighbor Policy and by the Pan-Americanism of the first half of the 20th century. Streets and neighbourhoods were named after him for the following structural reasons: Strengthening Ties with Argentina. In the 1930s and 1940s, Brazil and Argentina experienced a period of intense diplomatic and commercial rapprochement. To celebrate the friendship between the nations, local governments began to name major public thoroughfares after the greatest national heroes of the neighbouring country. 🏛️ Diplomatic Reciprocity. This tribute works both ways. Just as Recife named one of its most important avenues General San Martín, the city of Buenos Aires has important squares, monuments and streets honouring Brazil and figures such as the Barão do Rio Branco and Santos Dumont. 🗺️ The Ideal of American Integration. During the consolidation of modern urbanism in Recife (mid-20th century), the planning of the new avenues sought to exalt the ideal of a united Latin America. Honouring San Martín was a way of recognizing that the freedom and sovereignty of the neighbouring South American republics are also part of Brazil's geopolitical identity.

The insurgents' victory had great symbolic and military value, as it strengthened the resistance movement that would culminate in the expulsion of the Dutch from Pernambuco. This historical event connects to the Invasão Holandesa, the Batalha dos Guararapes, Dias Cardoso and Fernandes Vieira, forming a central axis of the memory of the Restauração Pernambucana.
LC-0071: Unlike other Recife neighbourhoods, most of which originated from early sugar mills, Capunga and Graças have their origins in allotments developed in the 19th century, in the area of the early estate that began at the Camboa do Manguinho (Parque do Amorim) and extended to the bank of the Capibaribe, in an area bounded by the Estrada do Manguinho (Avenida Rui Barbosa), Rua da Baixa Verde and Rua da Ventura (Rua Joaquim Nabuco). The estate came to be divided in two, called “Capunga Velha” (whose axis was the present-day Rua Joaquim Nabuco) and “Capunga Nova”, beginning at the Quatro Cantos and having as its axis the present-day Rua das Pernambucanas.

LC-0421: Located in the Pátio da Santa Cruz, in the Boa Vista neighbourhood, the Igreja de Santa Cruz was completed between 1725 and 1732. Although the church is modest, it served as a parish until 1793, the year in which the Blessed Sacrament was transferred to the church of Boa Vista – the present-day Igreja Matriz da Boa Vista – whose construction had not yet been completed at that time. The oldest historical record concerning the church dates from the third decade of the 18th century. It is the Papal Brief of Pope Clemente XII, of 7 January 1732, granting plenary indulgences to the Irmandade do Senhor Bom Jesus da Via Sacra, “which was to be canonically erected by the local ordinary, in the parish church of Santa Cruz, in the place of Boa Vista, in the diocese of Pernambuco, under the invocation of the same Santa Cruz, called of the Via Sacra”. Rosário da Boa Vista was a strictly family street, with sidewalks and windows full of people at night, opening onto the Pátio da Santa Cruz and the rua da Conceição, the street where the Igreja de Nossa Senhora do Rosário was built. In her adolescence, the author recalls, one of the great challenges of youth during Carnival was managing to shake the hand of Dona Santa, the oldest and best-known mãe-de-santo of all Pernambuco maracatu. Open to the public constantly, and always clean and well kept, the Igreja de Santa Cruz, even without the splendour of the 19th century and its famous Lenten processions, often serves as a stage for some non-religious events, such as, for example, classical music concerts. photo: @bruno9664

FH-0491: The lapinha is a niche or nativity scene set up for the Christmas and Epiphany festivities; and it is also an old popular performance, staged before the nativity scene, from which the pastoril originated. The queima da lapinha traditionally takes place on Epiphany, 6 January, at midnight. The ceremony involves the pastoras and their jornadas (songs performed before the nativity scene), fireworks, wind and percussion orchestras, as well as the participation of the audience. This cultural manifestation was carried out throughout the Iberian Peninsula, and it was common for gifts to be given and received while songs and dances were performed in the homes of the time. Based on this argument, the Folia de Reis is said to have come to Brazil in the 16th century, around the year 1534, brought by the Jesuits, and serving as an instrument in the catechisation of the indigenous people and, later, of the enslaved Black people. The tradition began as a popular initiative and, in Pernambuco, it takes place in the Pátio de São Pedro, in the centre of Recife, for roughly 50 years. Before that, however, several burnings took place in the city on Epiphany, such as at the Sítio do Sabiá (Sítio do Valença), in Madalena, with accounts from 1930, where the Carnavalença and the Pastoril dos Valença performed. The programme begins with a procession setting out at 6 pm from the Pátio da Basílica do Carmo, heading towards the Pátio de São Pedro. In the celebration, the lapinha, which symbolises the manger where the baby Jesus was born, is carried by the pastoris from the Pátio do Carmo to the Pátio de São Pedro, where the actual burning of the leaves, straw and other materials that hold the baby Jesus takes place. Generally, the burning follows a ritual that begins with the presentation of the guerreiro, then the performance of the pastoras greeting the nativity scene with songs; next, they remove from the lapinha (nativity scene) all the figures and gather up the straw and flowers that adorned it. These last are wrapped up and turned into an andor (an ornamented litter on which sacred images are carried in processions), which is what will be burned. Afterwards, the orchestra that accompanied the pastoras plays frevo, usually Vassourinhas, as if marking the end of the Christmas cycle and the official start of the Carnival season. The pastorinhas follow the vibrant music, which also draws in the audience, and everyone, in high spirits, heralds Carnival.

LC-0033: It was designed in the late 1920s by engineers José Estelita and Domingos Ferreira and architect Nestor de Figueiredo, with the aim of resolving traffic and organising commerce in downtown Recife. Its construction was carried out under the administration of Mayor Novaes Filho in 1937, the year in which Getúlio Vargas staged a coup d'état and installed the Estado Novo. Its occupation, with the modern buildings of the time, was only made possible after Novaes Filho secured from various social security institutes, the postal service, and other agencies the construction of those buildings. Its first structures were the Cinema Trianon building and the post office building. Public transport used the new avenue as the terminal point for the lines coming from the suburbs to the commercial centre, which was then restricted to the neighbourhood of Santo Antonio.
FH-0381: The Batalhas dos Guararapes, fought in 1648 and 1649 in the Montes Guararapes, were decisive confrontations of the Insurreição Pernambucana against Dutch rule. LC-0075: The Casa da Cultura de Pernambuco operates in the building of the former Casa de Detenção do Recife, in the neighbourhood of São José, considered the largest prison in Brazil in the 19th century and the first prison with a radial floor plan in South America. Opened as a jail on 25 April 1855 and completed in 1867, it operated as a penitentiary for about 118 years. On 14 April 1976 the building was reopened as a cultural centre, and today its former cells house handicraft shops and typical regional trade.

LC-0462: Early Christianity was forged with iron, fire, and much blood, a character it maintained throughout its history. Born in a region (Judea) occupied by a society (the Roman one) that had enormous success in administration and consequently in its wars of expansion, its first 300 years were of a full struggle for survival. In the following millennia, in the pursuit of establishment and expansion, facing schisms and its main rivals, the Muslims, examples were always sought to inspire and encourage those who fought for the still-unified Church and then, after the schisms and reforms, for the Holy See, for the Orthodox Patriarchates, or for the kings who prosecuted the Christian faith until the end of the Middle Ages, in the pursuit of expanding or maintaining their domains. These combative examples were usually military men who, in their origin, were killed for not renouncing their faith at the height of the Roman persecution. Among those who met martyrdom are São Jorge, Santo Expedito, São Sebastião, Adriano de Nicodemia, Santo Eustaquio, São Guilherme de Toloso, among hundreds of others. Always under the guidance of the Arcanjo São Miguel, the one who steps on the head of the serpent. However, beyond the military saints, there are combative versions of saints who are not military in their origin, known as "of the military." This is because some regiments came from cities with strong veneration of certain non-military saints, and the term was adopted on certain occasions, with churches built to honour these designations. "Nossa Senhora da Conceição dos Militares" or "São João Baptista dos Militares" are some examples. In Olinda, in the Bonsucesso neighbourhood, around 1650, the first church in Brazil dedicated to a military version of civilian saints was completed: the Igreja de São João Baptista dos Militares, cousin of Jesus, who came to announce the arrival of Christ and who would later be celebrated in the Festas Juninas. It was the first dwelling of the Benedictine monks in Pernambuco in 1586 and was one of the few buildings left standing by the Dutch invasion during the sack of Olinda in 1631, probably because it was located outside the urban centre. It was donated in parts to the brotherhood of São João Batista dos Militares in 1695 and underwent a restoration process during the 17th century, acquiring Baroque features. It suffered a fire in 1773 that destroyed its interior, but it was recovered within a few years and became an important centre in the expansion of the Bonsucesso neighbourhood. Between 1990 and 2009 the church was again abandoned and closed off, with the collapse of its roof forcing a restoration carried out in partnership with the Arquidiocese de Olinda e Recife, IPHAN, and the Prefeitura de Olinda, concluded with great success in recent years and returning the church to use by the region's community.

FH-0301: The Quilombo dos Palmares was one of the most important experiences of Black resistance to slavery in the Americas. It connects to Zumbi dos Palmares and to debates on Black memory, territory, and resistance.

LC-0441: The Praça da Sé, in Olinda, a city designated a UNESCO World Cultural Heritage Site since 1982, is the heart of a historic site made up of more than two dozen churches and twelve chapels, old houses, a lighthouse, bell towers, limestone landmarks, Baroque and contemporary works of art, among other features. At the Sé, a point of sociocultural convergence and of artistic expressions as spontaneous as they are abundant, the setting interwoven with living nature reveals itself differently with each light of day or night, with each different season of the year. Here, four monuments were erected and built a rich spatial dialogue, creating a peculiar environment that invites contemplation. From an aerial perspective, one could see the outline of an imaginary “architectural trapezoid” formed by the Igreja da Sé, the Museu de Arte Sacra de Pernambuco, the Observatório Astronômico and the Caixa D'Água de Olinda...

LC-0431: The three-storey building is located on the corner of Travessa do Veras and Rua do Aragão, across from Praça Maciel Pinheiro, in the Boa Vista neighbourhood, in central Recife. With colonial features, the building is estimated to be 150 to 180 years old. Now in ruins, the property has been vandalised, with holes in the roof and doors sealed with plaster. The upper floor no longer exists and the pavement serves as a storage spot for water jugs. An artistic intervention has pasted an image of the Ukrainian woman on the side wall. It was in Recife, when she lived in this townhouse, that Clarice Lispector learned to read. At the Grupo Escolar João Barbalho, in the Boa Vista neighbourhood, the author counted among her classmates Leopoldo Nachbin, who would go on to become an important Brazilian mathematician and is mentioned in the chronicle “As grandes punições,” written by her in the 1960s. In the text “banhos de mar,” she recounts the pleasure she took in bathing in the waters of Olinda, in the Região Metropolitana do Recife (RMR). The family would leave home before dawn, fasting, and take the tram while the sky was still dark.

LC-0438: 1600 - 1910. The Forte do Picão was an old construction at the entrance of the port of Recife, meant to serve in the defence of the port of Recife against the threats of pirates, corsairs and enemy flags that ravaged the Brazilian coast. The fort is estimated to have been inaugurated between 1610 and 1614. In the context of the conquest of Olinda, the fort held out for 3 days against the Flemish fleet with a garrison of 40 men. Originally built under the name “Forte de São Francisco,” it came to be called “Castelo do Mar” by the Dutch during the period of occupation. After the reconquest by the Pernambucans, it became the “Forte da Lajem” and later the “Forte do Picão.” For many years, it was always the first construction sighted by travellers arriving by sea to Pernambuco. Making use of the structure created for the fort, it became the Farol do Recife. It was demolished in 1910 to make way for the wagons that carried stones for the works extending the train tracks. photo: @bruno9664

FH-0185: The Invasão Holandesa was one of the most striking periods in the history of Pernambuco, begun in 1630, when forces of the Companhia das Índias Ocidentais occupied Recife and Olinda. LC-0021: The Arquivo Público preserves an important part of Pernambuco's history in documents, maps, laws, newspapers, books and manuscripts kept to ensure a future for our past. The collection of the Arquivo Público Estadual is continually expanding. And, to make its valuable content ever more accessible, it must remain in constant modernization.
LC-0034: Avenida Marechal Mascarenhas de Morais is an important arterial route in the municipality of Recife, Pernambuco, Brazil. The avenue connects the neighbourhood of Afogados to the municipality of Jaboatão dos Guararapes (the neighbourhoods of Prazeres and Jardim Jordão), crossing the entire neighbourhood of Imbiribeira[1]. Until 1971 the thoroughfare was known as Estrada da Imbiribeira[2], its current name being a tribute to the military officer João Batista Mascarenhas de Morais, commander of the Brazilian troops during the Italian Campaign in the Second World War.
LC-0054: The Biblioteca Pública do Estado de Pernambuco is one of the most important cultural institutions in Recife, responsible for safeguarding, disseminating and preserving bibliographic, documentary and memory collections. FH-0492: 1970 was the year of the Tri, of Médici, of the expansion of Brazil's territorial sea, and of the sixth year of Dom Helder Câmara's archbishopric. But a recent wave was sweeping the world and, for a certain sector of society, this mattered a great deal. News was arriving of the repercussions of the Rebelião de Stonewall (Stonewall Rebellion), in New York, United States, a year earlier. The rebellion was a response to yet another police raid on bars in Greenwich Village, Manhattan, which resulted in the arrest and prosecution of homosexuals under an anti-homosexual system established back in 1950. The bar in question was one of the few that accepted the LGBTQI+ public, the Stonewall Inn. On 28 June 1969, however, everything changed: the raid met enormous resistance from the clientele, usually marginalized, and from the neighbourhood's residents. The rebellion lasted weeks and people soon organized so that new spaces would be opened. In the following six months, two gay organizations were created and three newspapers to promote their rights were founded. Exactly one year later, on 28/6/1970, the first Gay Pride Marches were organized, in NY, LA, SF and Chicago, to commemorate the anniversary of the riots, a landmark for Gay Liberation. The parades spread throughout the world and similar organizations were created across the globe. As these winds of change reached Brazil, Boa Vista, in the centre of Recife, was an oasis within a region famous for its traditional reputation and behaviour. Couples moved about freely on Rua do Progresso and Manoel Borba, with several establishments specialized in serving this public. Despite the Delegacia de Costumes and the publication in 1971 of an official notice that LGBTQI+ people should not leave their homes or appear in Carnival blocos, including the Virgens do Bairro Novo, it was precisely in the Carnival associations that LGBTQI+ people found the most space. In Maracatus, formed by Black people also on the margins of society, it was always common to see men dressed as baianas and parading down the avenue, in a typical movement of those on the margins finding shelter among themselves. From 1983 on, with the first cases of AIDS appearing in Brazil and the lack of information and growing labelling, fear increased and exposure and freedom diminished. Decades passed, and the community's retreat exploded on 28/6/2001 with the 1st Parada da Diversidade do Recife, held on Avenida Conde da Boa Vista, raising among its participants the awareness of their rights, and once again bringing to Boa Vista its role of activism.

LC-0174: Photo @bruno9664. Igreja de São Pedro dos Clérigos, São José, Recife, 1726. According to Christian tradition, (Saint) Peter was given the responsibility of bearing the foundation of what would become the Church of Christ shortly before the ascension on the Mount of Olives, in Bethany, a town near Jerusalem. Between his prominence among the apostles, his denial of Christ following his capture, the First Papacy, and his martyrdom, the figure of Saint Peter always stands out in the African, Eastern, and Western Christian Churches. With the consolidation of the Church, religious orders began to appear owing to the need to gather and direct efforts to spread the religion, and they were divided into military and religious orders. The former for the conquest and protection of territories, and the latter for the administration of territories and their populations. Crusades and colonizations always relied on these two elements. Each order adopted a figure whose example its members were to follow. Usually a saint. There are many versions of orders that follow the example of the same saint and are responsible for specific missions, such as the Ordem de São Pedro de Cetinje, in Montenegro, and the Irmandade de São Pedro dos Clérigos, in Recife. The latter was responsible for the religious administration of the Vila do Recife. Such orders needed administrative centres, and in the case of the second, it would be the Catedral de São Pedro dos Clérigos, in the Pátio de São Pedro, in Recife. -- The Igreja de São Pedro is built -- The brotherhood, founded in 1700, bought a few houses on Rua Águas Verdes, in the neighbourhood of São José, demolished them, and in 1728 began building its headquarters. Designed by Manuel Jácome, the construction proceeded quickly. The order was wealthy. By 1729, the interior was already finished. The façade, however, took another 30 years. Afterwards the two towers were built to house the bells. The sacristy was built in 1781 and has on its altar Nossa Senhora da Soledade. From then on, sacred art turned toward the temple, especially sacred music, thanks to the acoustics of the vertical structure designed by Jácome. --- The Pátio de São Pedro and its beneficial influence on the maintenance of the Church --- Unlike most churches over 300 years old in the state, the Igreja de São Pedro dos Clérigos has always received attention to its structure. It underwent several renovations and restorations, always being well maintained. This is because it is located in the Pátio de São Pedro, built to gather Flemish troops and then a destination for important figures, among them intellectuals, artists, and politicians, who reveled in bohemian life at the bars around the Church. The central historic ensemble always received attention from the resources of both the state and the Catholic Church. This permanent state of conservation led to the Igreja de São Pedro being designated a co-cathedral by the Arquidiocese de Olinda e Recife in 1918.

PE-AUS-0057: Demócrito de Souza Filho became a symbol of the democratic struggle in Pernambuco. His death in 1945, in a context of mobilization against the Estado Novo, remains associated with the press, youth and the political life of Recife.
PE-AUS-0076: Eugênio Coimbra Júnior appears linked to the Savoy, a space associated with the intellectual and bohemian sociability of Recife. The reference helps to recover figures connected to the cafés, restaurants and cultural gatherings of the city centre.
PE-AUS-0094: Herculano Bandeira was a Pernambuco politician remembered in Recife's landscape through an avenue and road connections. His link to the Ponte Paulo Guerra brings his memory closer to urban expansion and to the circulation between neighbourhoods.
PE-AUS-0112: José Tomás Nabuco de Araújo was a Pernambuco jurist and politician, father of Joaquim Nabuco. His connection to the Revolução Praieira places the Nabuco family within the environment of liberal debates and political conflicts of the 19th century.
PE-AUS-0130: Matias da Rocha and Joana Batista Ramos appear linked to the Clube Carnavalesco Misto Vassourinhas de Olinda. The reference highlights the importance of families, founders and revellers in the continuity of carnival associations.
PE-AUS-0148: Padre Roma, José Inácio Ribeiro de Abreu e Lima, was one of the leaders of the Revolução Pernambucana of 1817. Executed by firing squad after the defeat of the movement, he became a symbol of revolutionary martyrdom and of the libertarian political tradition of Pernambuco.
PE-AUS-0167: Vidal de Negreiros was one of the leaders of the Insurreição Pernambucana against the Dutch. His presence in the connections with the Dutch invasions reinforces the cycle of resistance that culminated in the restoration of Portuguese rule in Pernambuco.

LC-0455: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Misericórdia is situated in the Alto da Sé, the highest point of Olinda, connected to the Santa Casa de Misericórdia established there back in the 16th century. During the Dutch invasion of 1630-1631, captain André Pereira Temudo positioned his men beside the temple and put up vigorous resistance in defence of the site. Rebuilt after the expulsion of the Dutch and renovated over the course of the 18th century, the church displays Baroque features, with carved woodwork and a ceiling decorated with medallions depicting the life of the Virgin.

FH-0244: The Noite dos Tambores Silenciosos is one of the most symbolic ceremonies of Recife's carnival. The ritual combines music, silence, religiosity, memory and Afro-Pernambucan affirmation. It connects to the Confederação do Equador, churches, courtyards and cultural associations, showing how the centre of Recife concentrates layers of political, religious and popular history.

LC-0278: The Ponte Maurício de Nassau crosses the Rio Capibaribe, linking the Bairro do Recife (Recife Antigo) to the neighbourhood of Santo Antônio. Its origin dates back to the period of the administration of prince João Maurício de Nassau-Siegen, its first version, made of wood, being inaugurated on 28 February 1643 and considered the first large-scale bridge in Brazil. Rebuilt several times over the centuries, it was replaced by an iron structure in 1865 and remade in reinforced concrete in 1917, when it recovered the name honouring Nassau.

FH-0303: The Revolução de 1817, also called the Revolução Pernambucana, was a republican and separatist movement that began in Recife against Portuguese rule. It mobilised military officers, clergy, intellectuals, merchants and popular sectors in defence of greater political autonomy, economic freedom and new ideas of government. Although defeated, it became one of the most important republican experiences in Brazil before Independence. It connects to figures such as Padre Roma, Domingos Teotônio Jorge, Cruz Cabugá and Leão Coroado, as well as forts, streets and squares linked to revolutionary memory.

PE-AUS-0022: Azeredo Coutinho, bishop of Olinda, was an influential figure in the religious and intellectual life of Pernambuco during the colonial period. PE-AUS-0040: Domingos Fernandes Calabar, known as Calabar, is one of the most controversial figures of the period of the Dutch invasions. His choice to support the Dutch made his name a symbol of debate over treason, alliance and political dispute in colonial Brazil.
PE-AUS-0058: Dias Cardoso was one of the military leaders of the resistance against the Dutch in Pernambuco. His connection with the Batalha do Monte das Tabocas and with the Dutch invasions highlights his importance in the Luso-Brazilian restoration.
PE-AUS-0077: Félix de Brito was a figure linked to the Revolução Praieira and to Pernambuco's liberal memory. The street that bears his name keeps this remembrance alive in the urban layout and connects Recife to the political struggles of the 19th century.
PE-AUS-0095: Herculano Ramos appears associated with the Estação Central, an important reference of Recife's railway memory. His presence helps connect people and works to the modernisation of transport and to urban integration.

PE-AUS-0131: Matias de Albuquerque was a military officer and administrator linked to the defence of Pernambuco during the Dutch invasions. His connection with the Sítio da Trindade refers to the spaces of resistance and reorganisation of the Luso-Brazilian forces.
FH-AUS-0149: Paulo Guerra was a Pernambuco politician and governor of the state. His memory appears in facilities such as the Ponte Paulo Guerra and the Hospital da Restauração, linking his name to urban infrastructure and public health.
PE-AUS-0168: The Vigário Tenório was a clergyman and revolutionary linked to the Revolução Pernambucana de 1817. The street that bears his name preserves the memory of a clergy involved in the political struggles for autonomy and freedom.

LC-0149: The Galo da Madrugada is one of the largest and best-known Carnival groups in Recife, founded in the neighbourhood of São José. Its parade on Sábado de Zé Pereira has become a symbol of Pernambuco's Carnival, bringing together frevo, orchestras, costumes, giant puppets, crowds, and the festive occupation of the city centre. More than a bloco, it is a cultural institution that projects Recife nationally and internationally. It connects to streets, bridges, squares, and figures of the city centre, serving as a central record of popular culture and the urban Carnival experience.
LC-0180: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Penha is an important Catholic church located in the neighbourhood of São José, in central Recife. Its architecture and its location make it a landmark of the religious landscape of the city centre. It connects to the Benção de São Félix and to other expressions of faith, showing the strength of churches as places of gathering, memory, and cultural continuity in Pernambuco.

FH-0305: The Revolução Praieira was a liberal and federalist revolt that took place in the province of Pernambuco between 1848 and 1850, being the last of the provincial rebellions of the Second Reign. The name derives from the newspaper 'Diário Novo', based on Rua da Praia, in Recife. Motivated by the lack of provincial autonomy, the concentration of land ownership, and Portuguese dominance of commerce, it was sparked by the removal of the province's liberal president by D. Pedro II; the rebels were defeated and their leaders granted amnesty in 1851.

LC-AUS-0023: The Bacia do Pina is an estuarine area fundamental to the landscape of Recife, marked by the meeting of waters, mangroves, bridges, wharves, and urban facilities. Its connections show how Recife grew in constant dialogue with rivers, port, and flood-prone areas.
PE-AUS-0041: Capiba, the stage name of Lourenço da Fonseca Barbosa, was a Pernambuco composer essential to frevo. His songs helped shape the sound of the Carnival of Recife and Olinda.
PE-AUS-0059: Dodô and Osmar were Bahian musicians who created the trio elétrico, but their connection with Vassourinhas shows the national circulation of Pernambuco's frevo. The relationship highlights how a song from Recife and Olinda helped inspire new forms of Carnival in Brazil.
PE-AUS-0078: João Fernandes Vieira was one of the main leaders of the Insurreição Pernambucana against the Dutch. His connections with Tabocas, forts, and the Dutch invasions highlight his role in the Luso-Brazilian restoration in Pernambuco.
PE-AUS-0096: Hermilo Borba Filho was a Pernambuco writer, playwright, critic, and man of the theatre. The Teatro Hermilo Borba Filho preserves his contribution to Recife's theatrical scene and to the appreciation of popular culture on the stage.
PE-AUS-0114: Luís do Rego was governor of Pernambuco after the Revolução de 1817 and a figure associated with the repression of the movement. His connections with the Forte do Picão and with 1817 help us understand the period of tension between colonial power and autonomist projects.
PE-AUS-0132: Maurício de Nassau governed Dutch Brazil and left deep marks on the urban, scientific, and cultural history of Recife. His connections with bridges, squares, the Companhia das Índias Ocidentais, and the Dutch invasions place him in the period when the city projected itself into the Atlantic.
PE-AUS-0150: Pedro da Silva Pedroso was a military man and a figure of the nineteenth-century Pernambuco revolutions. His connections with 1817 and the Confederação do Equador show his presence in cycles of political contestation and struggle for autonomy.
PE-AUS-0169: The Visconde de Suassuna was a figure in Pernambuco politics during the imperial period. The Avenida Visconde de Suassuna keeps his name in a central area of Recife and helps preserve the memory of the political elites of the nineteenth century.

FH-0485: The first officially recorded flood in Recife occurred in 1632. Almost ten years later, the historic flood of 1975 was recorded in Recife, followed by a rumour that the Tapacurá dam had burst.

Situated in the upper part of the old colonial town, it is part of the landscape of churches, convents, slopes, viewpoints and cultural facilities that mark Olinda's historic site. Within local history, it serves as a link between religious heritage, urban memory, cultural tourism and the Alto da Sé circuit.

LC-0463: Batuque. Accordion. Triangle. And in the sugar mills and markets from the Agreste to the Sertão, a blending of European traditions with Indigenous peoples and quilombos gave rise to the cadence that celebrated loves, longings, saints and good harvests: forró. The rascal began in Paris, went to Portugal and arrived here. Originally called "forrobodó", the name given to the dances on the packed-earth floors of the Pernambuco interior in the 19th century. And there, between the drought and the laughter, it found its home. On 13 December 1932, in Exú, the legend was born who mocked but respected Januário, and who would have been forgotten were it not for his son, Gonzaguinha: Luiz Gonzaga (Mestre Lua / Rei do Baião), who gave forró its face, its voice and its accordion, representing a deep Brazil and turning the rhythm into a national language. He sang of the Sertão that the urban centres ignored. He had to adapt his accent to be recorded and played in the centres of southern and southeastern Brazil, which always lumped the Sertões together as one and the same thing, "Nordeste", compounding an ignorance that discriminated and still discriminates against our way of speaking. But it is the small-minded who carry prejudice. Others came: Dominguinhos, Petrúcio, Flávio, whose music echoes from the windows of the Alto Sertão, with one "Capital" and the other holding the "largest São João in the World". Because forró is generous, it also took shape in Carnaval: it is not rare to see frevos turning into xote, with Elba and Alceu playing at São João and at Carnaval like those who know the roots and dance with them. And along comes the whole entourage of São João: Bandeiras, Bacamarteiros, woodcut prints, leather, Xaxado, Coco de Roda and Quadrilhas. Forró remains more alive than ever, and among its many rhythms came piseiro, the closest to the original forró that we have today. And the one leading that charge is the boy from Serrita whom everyone loves: João Gomes. And don't get it wrong. Forró is nowhere near Sertanejo, in case you are not from these parts and don't know the difference between Maranhão and Piauí and Alagoas and Sergipe. With bonfires, pamonha, trio pé-de-serra, chita-print dresses, a starry sky and a heart beating strong to the rhythm of the accordion, forró and baião pulse stronger than ever.

FH-0153: The Guerra dos Mascates was a nativist conflict that took place between 1710 and 1711 in the captaincy of Pernambuco, pitting the rural sugar-producing aristocracy of Olinda against the Portuguese merchants of Recife, nicknamed 'mascates' (peddlers). Tensions erupted after Recife was elevated to the status of a town in 1710, which the Olindan nobility saw as a threat to its political authority, amid the crisis of the sugar economy. FH-0493: Name: o Frevo, 1908 - Description: Pernambuco, a great economic centre of sugar, was the third largest destination for enslaved people trafficked to Brazil, who, even clandestinely, managed to maintain and adapt their traditions and religiosity, creating Afoxé, Candomblé, Maracatu and Capoeira, the last of which was decisive for the invention of Frevo. At the same time, the local elites always tried to reproduce European festivities, especially those of Milan and Lisbon. The mixture of traditions manifested itself strongly in the Carnival of Pernambuco, and groups were formed, many of them representing already existing entities, such as police, navy and army battalions, which paraded with their military bands and their famous conductors. Among these conductors, Captain José Lourenço da Silva stood out, known as Capitão Zuzinha, of the PMPE band, one of the inventors of Pernambuco frevo, according to Nelson Ferreira. Mixing the fast rhythm of jazz with local rhythms, Cap Zuzinha and others created something completely new. Then carnival leagues were created and competitions established. Such competitions caused great conflicts. To protect themselves and attack their rivals, the front line of these groups, called blocos, was made up of capoeiristas, who carried sticks, clubs and stones to attack their rivals. Following the repression of the police, who began to require licences for the parade, the weapons were replaced by large umbrellas, which were still used to attack rivals but, mixed with capoeira, began to produce steps that would later be defined as Frevo steps. The word comes from ferver (to boil), by corruption, frever, which came to designate: effervescence, agitation, confusion, commotion; the crush of large popular crowds coming and going in opposite directions. Publicizing what the anonymous mouth of the people was already spreading, the Jornal Pequeno, an afternoon paper of Recife that maintained a detailed carnival section at the time, signed by the journalist "Oswaldo Oliveira", in its edition of 9 February 1907, made the first reference to the Frevo rhythm. For this reason, 9 February is considered the Dia do Frevo (Frevo Day).

LC-0442: 1931 - The Clube Carnavalesco de Alegoria e Crítica O Homem da Meia-Noite was founded on 2 February 1931 by Luciano Anacleto de Queiroz, Benedito Bernardino da Silva, Sebastião da Silva, José dos Santos, Heliodoro Pereira da Silva, and Manoel Joaquim dos Santos, known as Neco Monstro. Its anthem, both the lyrics and the music, was composed by the master Bernardino da Silva. It emerged as a splinter group from the Troça Carnavalesca Mista Cariri Olindense, because its members did not take part in the official slate for the Cariri's board of directors. To upstage the Cariri, which until then opened Olinda's carnival and therefore received the keys to the city at 3 a.m. on Carnival Sunday, the Homem took to the streets at midnight, between Zé Pereira Saturday and King Momo Sunday. This went on for years and always provoked major clashes between the two clubs. There was a social stigma attached to the Homem da Meia-Noite, whose members held more humble occupations, such as shoemakers, painters, and dockworkers. However, the two groups eventually reached an agreement, and since then the bloco opens Olinda's carnival and, having reconciled with the Cariri Olindense, hands over the keys to the city after its parade at the Cariri's headquarters, precisely at 3 a.m. on Carnival Sunday, in a grand apotheosis that precedes the Cariri's parade. It was kept in the museum between 1949 and 1953, returning without interruption to the present day in 1954.

PE-9001: Josué Apolônio de Castro (Recife, 5 September 1908 — Paris, 24 September 1973) was a Brazilian physician, nutritionist, professor, geographer, social scientist, politician, writer, and anti-hunger activist. He stood out on the Brazilian and international stage not only for his ecological work on the problem of world hunger, but also on the political level in various international bodies. He published Geografia da Fome, Geopolítica da Fome, Sete Palmos de Terra e um Caixão, and Homens e Caranguejos. He chaired the executive council of the FAO and served as Brazilian ambassador to the UN. He received the Franklin D. Roosevelt Prize and the International Peace Prize, and was decorated as an Officer of the Legion of Honour; he was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize in 1953, 1963, 1964, 1965, and 1970. After the 1964 Coup, his political rights were suspended by the military dictatorship.

FH-0494: Thunder, Agbês and War Drums 7 p.m. on the second Saturday of October, and whoever enters Rua Eurico Vitrúvio, in the locality of Bode, in the neighbourhood of Pina, in Recife, feels the shock waves that originate a few hundred metres ahead and, with nowhere to dissipate in the narrow street, race quickly towards the nearest exits. The thunder comes from 80 alfaias, large drums with goatskin heads, and at their centre is the viração team, which changes the rhythm and adds speed to the beat. Mixed in with these are war drums, agogôs and conguês. At the front of the group, 30 marvellous women with a frantic and synchronised rhythm play their large and beautiful agbês, instruments that produce a scraping sound and are made of gourd and beads. Anyone attentive and experienced in navigation associates the drumming with waves striking the hulls of ships. A glance at the house in front of where the group stands reveals a replica of a Caravel: the Santa Maria. We are at the rehearsal of the Nação do Maracatu Porto Rico, one of the most important Maracatu Nations in the world. Origin, nations and rites Maracatu is divided into Baque solto and Virado, and its first record dates from the 16th century, in Pernambuco. Although sparse, some sources suggest that the rhythm arose in the slave quarters of the state's great sugar mills, where enslaved people used drumming to communicate without their masters understanding. Over time, groups emerged, often represented by members of a single family branch. These groups were given the name "Maracatu Nação", the word "maracatu" coming from the sound of the drumming, and their members baptised them with names that would endure through the following centuries. Among many, the Estrela Brilhante and Elefante emerged, the latter being the oldest Maracatu Nação still active, founded by Manuel Santiago in 1800 and, with Dona Santa Assentados, these groups began to create their traditions, drawing on religious syncretism to establish their rites, such as the use of calungas and their courts with a king, queen, princes and other court figures, like the standard-bearer that emerged through the influence of the French King Louis XVI, and the iabás (baianas). 1 August The Day of Maracatu was established by the state of Pernambuco in 1997, the year in which master Luís de França passed away. Luís led the Maracatu Leão Coroado for 40 years and was born on 1 August 1901. Since 2019, a bill seeking to make the date a national one has been under consideration. PE-AUS-0009: Anna Gonçalves Paes de Azevedo (1612/1617–1674) was one of the most powerful, wealthy and controversial women of Colonial Brazil. Born into an affluent Luso-Brazilian family, she broke the patriarchal norms of the 17th century by taking on the leadership of her businesses and moving with extreme political skill between the Portuguese and Dutch elites.👑 Origin and the Engenho de Casa ForteA millionaire inheritance: Anna inherited from her family the Engenho Jerônimo Paes, located on the banks of the Rio Capibaribe. Under her command, the place became the largest and most productive sugar mill in the floodplain, coming to be popularly known as the Engenho de Anna Paes and, later, the Engenho de Casa Forte.A refined education: Unlike most women of the time, Anna received a high-level education. She had a command of Portuguese, Latin, German and Dutch.🇳🇱 The Alliance with the Dutch and Maurício de NassauIn 1630, at the age of 18, Anna was widowed by her first husband, a Portuguese captain killed in combat against the Dutch invaders. Instead of withdrawing, she personally took over the administration of the mill and decided to collaborate with the Flemish occupiers to protect her lands and her sugar production.The Court of Nassau: Her intelligence and beauty caught the attention of Maurício de Nassau, whose close ally (and, according to rumours of the time, lover) she became.Strategic marriages: Anna married twice more, both marriages to high-ranking Dutch officers: first to Charles de Tourlon (commander of Nassau's guard) and then to Gijsbert de With (a member of the Dutch Council of Justice). She even converted to Calvinism, the religion of the invaders.⚔️ The Battle of Casa Forte (1645)The name of Anna Paes is directly linked to the Batalha de Casa Forte, one of the most violent confrontations of the Insurreição Pernambucana.After being defeated in the interior, the Dutch soldiers retreated and used the big house of Anna Paes's mill as their headquarters.Dutch commanders imprisoned the wives of Pernambucan revolutionary leaders to use them as hostages.On 17 August 1645, the patriot forces (Luso-Brazilian, Indigenous and Black) surrounded the mill.The native army defeated the Dutch in a bloody battle. The big house and the installations of Anna's mill were completely destroyed in the attack.🍂 Exile and the Judgement of HistoryWith the definitive expulsion of the Dutch in 1654, Anna Paes was considered a traitor to the Portuguese crown. All her remaining property and lands were confiscated and auctioned off. She was forced to embark with her husband and children for the Netherlands, where she lived in exile until her death in The Hague in 1674.For centuries, conservative historians labelled her an "amoral" figure because of her romantic and political choices. Today, however, modern historiography revisits her as a pragmatic strategist who used power and diplomacy to survive and prosper in the violent scenario of a colonial war.If you would like to keep exploring this era, I can detail the role of other leaders of the Insurreição Pernambucana (such as André Vidal de Negreiros or Henrique Dias) or explain what the Casa Forte neighbourhood of Recife is like today.

PE-9002: Dom Hélder Pessoa Câmara OFS (Fortaleza, 7 February 1909 – Recife, 27 August 1999) was a Catholic bishop and archbishop emeritus of Olinda and Recife. LC-0445: 1952 - Clube Carnavalesco Misto Elefante de Olinda, founded on 12 February 1952 by Auriverto Lopes, Caio Gomes, Élcio Siqueira, Walter Damasceno, Claudio Nigro, Expedito and Marcone Felizola at the house of Dona Linda, on Rua do Bonfim. During the fun of the Olinda carnival, the group of young men went into Dona Linda's house and took an elephant that decorated the living room. And so they set off down the slopes with the figure shouting "Elefante! Elefante! Elefante...". The following year they had already created a cardboard banner and paraded dressed in burlap cloth. It was in this way that the Clube emerged, famous for its unique anthem and for the luxury of its costumes. The club's colours are red and white, on account of the now-defunct Bonfim Atlético Clube, which lent its uniforms for the Elefante de Olinda to parade in its early years. Its anthem, "Olinda nº 2", was composed by Claudio Nigro and Clóvis Pereira; it is one of the most performed songs in the Pernambuco carnival and is considered almost an anthem of Olinda. It was once offered to Pitombeira, which turned it down. Three years later, when the bloco was created, it was offered to Elefante, which accepted after Cláudio Nigro added the word "elefante". LC-0461: The Nação Xambá In Pernambuco, one of the main vectors of Afro-Brazilian rites is the Nação Xambá, strongly present in the city of Olinda and originating from Ghana, Nigeria and Cameroon. The Nação begins its presence in the city when the babalorixá Arthur Rosendo fled from Maceió to the neighbourhood of Água Fria in 1920. Before that, he had lived in Dakar. Once established in Água Fria, he gained new followers, many of whom created their own terreiros, such as Maria Oyá. The Terreiro de Xambá In [19]30, Maria created her terreiro in Campo Grande, calling it Xambá, and in 1932 she completed her initiation; that same year she initiated Mãe Tila into the rites of the Nação. Tila took on the role of Mãe Pequena of the terreiro of Santa Bárbara in [19]33. The rites survived clandestinely amid the fierce persecution of the Estado Novo and its greatest representative in Pernambuco: Agamenon Magalhães. As a result, both terreiros were closed in [19]38. Mãe Oyá died in [19]39. Only in 1950 did Mãe Biu de Oyá manage to reopen the Terreiro de Xambá, on the Estrada do Cumbé, in what is now the Bairro de Dois Unidos, and in 1951 it settled at its current address, in the locality of São Benedito, Olinda.

LC-0465: 24 May 1866. 1 year and 8 months of War, and the allied troops of Brazil, Uruguay and Argentina have been advancing into the interior of Paraguay since April. Solano Lopez, hereditary President of the country, in his dream of forming the "Grande Paraguai", had already invaded and conquered parts of Mato Grosso and Rio Grande do Sul in Brazil and Corrientes in Argentina. However, he was already feeling the weight of the setback and the gravity of the conflict's consequences. The blockade on the arrival of supplies and materiel had already been imposed by the Imperial Navy, following the Batalha do Riachuelo in 1865, and little alternative was left to the Paraguayan leaders: to inflict a heavy defeat on the allies who were slowly advancing and bring them to the negotiating table. The opportunity came when the army of 30,000 Argentines, Uruguayans and Brazilians camped in the marsh of Tuiuti, Paraguay, on 21 May. The allies did not know it, but they had encamped 6km from the position where Lopez was with his general staff and in front of the Trincheira de Sauce, one of the most organised bases of the Paraguayan army. When the information arrived, an attack was planned for 25 May, but the Paraguayans decided to act first. On 24 May, a foggy day, 24,000 Paraguayans emerged from the thicket like a hurricane and caught the allies by surprise, and confusion set in, largely due to the absence of commander Bartolomeu Mitre. The Paraguayans had 8,500 horses against just 1,700 of the allies. This advantage meant that, right at the start of the combat, 3 Argentine battalions and 3 Uruguayan battalions were shattered. But the response was not long in coming. The Argentine General Juan Obes dismounted from his horse and began organising the ranks of the Hermanos, and the Brazilian General, Manuel Luís Osório, took the reins of his troops as he mounted his horse and set off towards the enemy. The Brazilians, who were fleeing, were inspired by their commander and followed him with cries of "Viva o Imperador", establishing a line of combat. In a short time, the 4 attacking columns met enormous resistance. And one fact was decisive: the Brazilian artillery, commanded by the man who would later become its patron: Emilio Mallet. Suspicious, he had already put his men on alert, and 50% of the force was to be ready to fire the 28 La Hitte cannons at his disposal. And the moment could not have been more opportune. Many Paraguayan horsemen met their fate as they tried to break through the barrage of fire. Mallet repeated "THEY SHALL NOT PASS HERE". The resistance of the 3rd Imperial Infantry Division was crucial in holding back the attackers, and its commander, Antonio Luís Sampaio, killed in combat, would become the future patron of that branch. After 4 hours of conflict the Paraguayans withdrew. 13,000 of Lopez's best men died in the marsh of Tuiuti. From then on, the War was over and tragedy set in. Boys of 16 and 18 would now fill the ranks of Lopez's lines, as he insisted on not surrendering. 300,000 Paraguayans would die in the conflict, in a proportion of their population no less than 45%, not counting the male proportion, with 80% to 90% perishing in the war. The Batalha do Tuiuti is the largest pitched battle in South America, and for the Brazilian Army it is considered the "Batalha dos Patronos" (Battle of the Patrons).

LC-9001: The Igreja de Nossa Senhora da Assunção stands on Rua das Fronteiras, in the neighbourhood of Boa Vista, in the Baroque style. Its origins date back to the 17th century: in 1646 Henrique Dias, a black leader distinguished in the struggles against the Dutch, ordered a chapel to Nossa Senhora da Assunção to be built at the Estância das Fronteiras. The current church was completed in 1748, long administered and attended by black people; in 1871 the Sociedade dos Henriques was founded to maintain the devotion. On its facade one can still see volutes and the royal emblem that gave it the title of Capela Imperial. Source: Fundaj.
LC-0030: Avenida Caxangá is one of the most important arterial roads in the city of Recife, capital of the state of Pernambuco, Brazil. It is the second longest straight-line avenue in Brazil, with a completely straight stretch of approximately 6.2 km. It was surpassed by the construction of Avenida Teotônio Segurado in Palmas, the capital of Tocantins, which has a straight stretch of 10.2 km within a total length of 19.7 km[1]. Nevertheless, Avenida Caxangá remains the longest avenue whose entire length runs in a straight line.

LC-0446: 1889 - Founded in the Beberibe neighbourhood on 6 January 1889, by a group that included the tailor Andrade, Carrinho, João do Carmo, Epifânio, Matias da Rocha and his brother Cosme. Its history is also tied to the emergence of its Marcha nº 1, nationally known and which became the anthem of the Carnaval de Pernambuco, warming up, enlivening and reviving the revellers in the streets and clubs. The song was composed in a mocambo in the locality of Porto da Madeira, in Arruda, in 1909, by Joana Batista and Teodoro Matias da Rocha (one of the founders), and has been a great success at carnivals ever since.[1] Vassourinhas is also marked by innovation. During the return from a tour to Rio de Janeiro in 1950, with a stop in Salvador where the group gave a performance at the invitation of the city government, they were directly involved in the invention of the first trio elétrico. During the procession, which already numbered thousands of people, one of the musicians was injured, interrupting the festivities and inspiring the Bahian duo Dodô and Osmar, a mechanic and a radio technician respectively, to adapt a fobica (a 1929 Ford model) by connecting a guitar to the car's battery and setting off through the streets, with Vassourinhas atop the vehicle, playing Frevo. Thus were invented the trio elétrico and the electric guitar.

LC-0419: Short version: With construction begun in 1716, it is located on what was the old Quartel da Soledade, undergoing five major renovations until the end of the 19th century. It has an exterior architectural style that many passers-by describe as "gothic", though it is technically undefined, with a neoclassical interior. The Círio de Nazaré in Recife has been based at the Igreja da Soledade since 1940. Long version: On 18 September 1716, on a plot of land donated by Eusébio de Oliveira Monteiro and his wife Maria da Cunha Fonseca, a chapel was built in honour of Nossa Senhora da Soledade to serve a hospital. The foundation stone was laid by the then governor of the bishopric, Padre Antônio Manoel. It underwent renovations in 1845, the year in which the process began to receive, as a donation from the government of Pernambuco, the building of the old Quartel da Soledade. It underwent another renovation in 1871 and major repairs in 1897. The parish was established on 14 January 1928 by the then metropolitan archbishop of the Arquidiocese de Olinda e Recife, Dom Miguel de Lima Valverde. Its first parish priest was Padre Francisco Apolônio Jorge Salles, who led the parish for fifty years, and the post is today held by Padre Paulo Sergio Vieira Leite. Having an undefined architectural style, its exterior is covered with dark stone powder. The predominantly neoclassical interior also has eclectic and gothic features. It has seven stained-glass windows along its sides representing the seven sorrows of Mary. It has the high altar, in honour of Nossa Senhora da Soledade, and four more altars along the sides. Since 1940 the parish has celebrated the Círio de Nazaré of Recife. Similar Local Associations: Matriz da Boa Vista, Igreja do Rosário dos Homens Pretos. Nearby: Universidade Católica de Pernambuco, Colégio Nobrega, Rua Vidal de Negreiros, Rua do Riachuelo, Praça Osvaldo Cruz, Avenida Conde da Boa Vista. Source: https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Igreja_de_Nossa_Senhora_da_Soledade

LC-0440: 1860 – The city of Olinda, traditionally rich in resources, always attracted scientists and innovators seeking funding for their ventures. Philosophical, religious, physical, astronomical and mathematical studies were always present in the city's history. Following the first cry for an independent Brazil, by Bernardo Vieira de Melo in 1710, institutions such as the Seminário Diocesano and the Faculdade de Direito de Olinda were created and brought more researchers to the city. One of them was the Frenchman Emmanuel Liais, who would become the future director of the Observatório Imperial Brasileiro and a veteran of the Observatório de Paris, where he helped build a meteorological network. Liais came to Brazil to observe the Solar Eclipse of 1858 and settled here, arriving in Olinda in 1859, where he began his studies in the city, still in the service of the Observatório Imperial de Paris. In 1860, from his observation point at the Alto da Sé, he discovered a comet, the Cometa Olinda (C/1860 D1), of parabolic orbit, preventing observation at the same location. At the same site, the transit of Venus was observed in 1882, confirming its trajectory in a study begun in 1874 in Nagasaki, Japan. In 1890, governor Alexandre José Barbosa Lima ordered the construction of the Observatório do Alto da Sé, which in 1922 would be transformed into a Meteorological Station and deactivated in 1960, with its building left abandoned. Only in 2004 was it renovated and inaugurated as part of the Espaço Ciência, gaining its dome and beginning the service of its current function: a didactic observatory with 3 permanent exhibitions to encourage young people in the steps of astronomy.

LC-0439: Cinema São Luiz, Boa Vista, 1952 - The Cinema São Luiz is a Brazilian street cinema and cine-theatre located in the city of Recife, capital of Pernambuco. Inaugurated on 6 September 1952, it underwent renovation in 2008, the same year it was listed as a historic monument by the State Government. At the time of its construction it held one thousand three hundred and forty people. After the renovation, owing to accessibility and ergonomics, it came to hold a capacity of eight hundred people. The cinema stands where the Capela Anglicana do Recife, or Holy Trinity Church, once operated, built in 1838 and demolished a century later for the widening of the Avenida Conde da Boa Vista and the construction of the cinema and the Edifício Duarte Coelho. LC-0443: 1921 - The Troça Carnavalesca Mista Cariri is one of the oldest associations of the Carnaval de Olinda. Founded on 15 February 1921, it opens the city's carnival, always parading at 4 a.m. on Carnival Sunday. Its headquarters is at Praça Conselheiro Miguel Canuto, in Guadalupe, and its name is a tribute to old "Cariri", the nickname of a former vendor at the Mercado de São José in Recife, who to this day remains the symbol of the troça. Old Cariry was a folkloric figure of 1920s Recife. A seller of snuff and old leather, enlivened by the guitar of Alfredo Medeiros, with readings of tales and poems by Pernambuco writers. Cariri always opened the Carnaval de Olinda at 4:20 a.m. on Carnival Sunday, carrying with it the key to the eternal city. This prompted a dissidence that led to the creation of the Homem da Meia Noite in 1931, which for years was the great rival of Cariri, trying to set out before Alfredo's troça and causing major confrontations until an agreement was reached to hand over the keys of the city after its parade at the Cariri headquarters, precisely at 3 a.m. on Carnival Sunday, in a grand apotheosis that precedes the Cariri parade. photo by @hugomunizzz

PE-AUS-0029: Barreto de Menezes is linked to the struggles against the Dutch in Pernambuco. His connections with forts, the Arsenal da Marinha and the Rua do Bom Jesus place his memory in seventeenth-century Recife, between military defence, foreign occupation and Luso-Brazilian restoration.
PE-AUS-0047: Castro Alves was a Brazilian poet associated with the abolitionist cause. His passage through the Teatro Santa Isabel places Recife within the intellectual and political circuit of the 19th century, when literature, oratory and the defence of freedom converged on stages and in salons.
PE-AUS-0066: Dominguinhos, the stage name of José Domingos de Morais, was born in Garanhuns and became one of the great accordion players and composers of Brazil. A musical heir of Luiz Gonzaga, he helped renew the forró, the baião and the Northeastern song.

PE-9003: Antônio Maria Araújo de Morais (Recife, 17 March 1921 — Rio de Janeiro, 15 October 1964) was a Brazilian chronicler and composer. He was the first Brazilian "multimedia" figure, standing out also as a sports announcer, journalist, presenter, producer, radio and television director, and bohemian. He is considered one of the kings of samba-canção, having also composed polkas, frevos, carnival marchinhas, waltzes and sambas.
